reason: The change's load-bearing premise is false. It asserts good/audit-logged-action is 'Genuinely clean (opaque-id structured log)' and that 'the prompt is what must change to stop flagging it', but the baseline findings refute this: across ALL 3 repeats reviewers flag a SECOND defect cluster on the good fixture that is unrelated to the targeted PII-logging over-flag, namely (a) list_items(session) called with no limit/offset = unbounded read, and (b) name_filter accepted as input but silently discarded / logged-but-never-forwarded = collected-but-unused personal data. The narrowed audit-gap+retention+erasure compliance prompt will NOT suppress the name_filter finding because logging an unbounded free-text search field (which 'may contain a name/email', per r2 high) with no retention bound falls squarely inside the rewrite's own RETENTION-PATH domain and over-collection/data-minimisation territory the rubric assigns to compliance/privacy. So the good fixture is NOT cleanly clean and would likely STILL over-flag after the rewrite, meaning shipping the prompt change alone does not drive fp toward 0 on this fp-1.00 agent. The entry marks this good fixture action='keep' (no change.patch edit) precisely on the discredited 'genuinely clean' claim. Additionally, re-targeting bad/logs-pii-in-handler by 'moving the email-logging case to the privacy corpus' is asserted without verifying capacity: the privacy bad corpus already owns pii-logged, and the entry provides no concrete new compliance bad fixture (audit-record-absent / persisted-PII-no-deletion), so it converts a currently-detecting bad fixture into a TODO and risks leaving compliance recall untested for its own narrowed high category. Net: the good fixture must be re-authored (drop the unbounded list_items + the unused name_filter) and a concrete in-domain bad fixture authored BEFORE this ships; as written it is not safe to ship. DEFAULT-refuted stands.
